prompt:
ADS-B Exchange snapshot extracted from acas_20241201.csv.gz. Two aircraft were recorded at 2024-12-01T22:57:15Z near Oakland, CA. TCAS Resolution Advisories were active on both aircraft.

Aircraft 1 — hex a5a2cc: Position 37.5639°N 122.2445°W, Altitude 1,775 ft, TCAS RA active (Monitor vertical Speed)
Aircraft 2 — hex 406c39: Position 37.5661°N 122.2405°W, Altitude 2,375 ft, TCAS RA active (Clear of Conflict)

Both aircraft are in Class B terminal airspace near KOAK. This is a real trajectory snapshot from ADS-B Exchange (source file: acas_20241201.csv.gz, timestamp 22:57:15–22:58:57 UTC, 2024-12-01).

Using the separation_calculator (haversine) tool, compute horizontal and vertical separation. Then identify all hazards in this snapshot.
gold_decision:
TOOL CALL: separation_calculator(lat1_deg=37.5639, lon1_deg=-122.2445, lat2_deg=37.5661, lon2_deg=-122.2405)
RESULT: distance_nm = 0.232 NM.

HAZARD ANALYSIS:
1. LOSS OF SEPARATION (Critical): Horizontal separation 0.232 NM is far below the 3 NM terminal radar minimum (FAA JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b). Vertical separation 600 ft is also below the 1,000 ft minimum.
2. SIMULTANEOUS DUAL STANDARD VIOLATION: Both horizontal (<3 NM) and vertical (<1,000 ft) separation minima are violated simultaneously — a full loss of separation.
3. TCAS RA ACTIVE: TCAS RA 'Monitor vertical Speed' on ac a5a2cc indicates ACAS is actively managing the conflict. ATC must NOT issue conflicting clearances per JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29.
4. MANDATORY REPORT: Loss-of-separation event requires mandatory occurrence reporting per JO 7110.65Z §2-1-2.
Source: ADS-B Exchange acas_20241201.csv.gz, 2024-12-01T22:57:15–22:58:57Z.
required_safety_constraints: Horizontal separation 0.232 NM << 3 NM terminal minimum — loss of separation confirmed
Vertical separation 600 ft < 1,000 ft minimum — second violation layer
ATC must not issue instructions conflicting with active TCAS RA per FAA JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29
Mandatory occurrence report required for confirmed loss of separation
acceptable_variants: Haversine result may show 0.23–0.24 NM depending on rounding; accept ±0.01 NM
evidence_requirements: ADS-B Exchange acas_20241201.csv.gz timestamp 22:57:15Z — real trajectory snapshot
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b — terminal 3 NM horizontal minimum
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29 — TCAS RA ATC procedures

Aircraft 2 — hex abdd63: Position 37.5850°N 122.2856°W, Altitude 1,125 ft

TOOL CALL: separation_calculator(lat1_deg=37.5833, lon1_deg=-122.2922, lat2_deg=37.5850, lon2_deg=-122.2856)
RESULT: distance_nm = 0.330 NM.

HAZARD ANALYSIS:
1. SEVERE HORIZONTAL LOS: 0.330 NM horizontal separation is 9× below the 3 NM terminal minimum. Extreme proximity event.
2. VERTICAL NEAR-MISS: 1,125 - 1,050 = 75 ft vertical separation — essentially co-altitude. Vertical minimum is 1,000 ft. Both minima simultaneously violated.
3. COORDINATED TCAS RA: Climb RA on one aircraft and Descend RA on the other is TCAS coordination — system determined opposite vertical maneuvers are needed. This is the highest TCAS alert level.
4. LOW ALTITUDE COMPOUNDING: At 1,050–1,125 ft, both aircraft are in low-level terminal airspace with minimal margin for maneuver. Climb RA at 1,050 ft requires immediate compliance.
5. ATC MUST REMAIN SILENT: During coordinated TCAS RA, ATC must not issue altitude instructions per 14 CFR §91.227 and JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29.

75 ft vertical separation << 1,000 ft minimum — co-altitude condition
Coordinated Climb/Descend RA requires zero ATC interference per 14 CFR §91.227

prompt:
ADS-B Exchange real operations data from operations_20241201.csv.gz. Two aircraft made same-runway (RWY 28) landings at KATL on 2024-12-01, recorded by ADS-B Exchange.

Landing sequence:
Flight 1 — DAL1882 (B752, Heavy, registration N696DL): Landed KATL RWY 28 at 2024-12-01T00:00:37Z
Flight 2 — DAL1720 (B752, Heavy, registration N685DA): Landed KATL RWY 28 at 2024-12-01T00:01:07Z

In-trail spacing interval: 30 seconds between touchdown times. Approach speed typical for B752: ~140 kt on final.

Source: operations_20241201.csv.gz, 2024-12-01T00:00:37–00:01:07Z.

Identify all hazards in this landing sequence. What is the effective in-trail separation at touchdown for a 30-second gap at 140 kt approach speed?
gold_decision:
IN-TRAIL SEPARATION ANALYSIS:

1. CALCULATING TRAILING DISTANCE: At 140 kt approach speed, distance = 140 × (30/3600) = 1.167 NM at touchdown.

2. WAKE TURBULENCE MINIMUM: Two B752 (Heavy) aircraft in sequence. FAA wake turbulence separation for Heavy following Heavy on same runway: minimum 4 NM in-trail on final approach per FAA JO 7110.65Z §3-9-6 wake turbulence tables. 1.167 NM << 4 NM — WAKE TURBULENCE SEPARATION VIOLATED.

3. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY: With 30-second spacing at 140 kt, the first aircraft has covered only ~1.17 NM from touchdown. Clearing the runway takes 30–60 seconds. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY HAZARD: second aircraft may land on occupied runway.

4. ADS-B DATA SIGNIFICANCE: Real KATL overnight operations showing tight spacing — even at 00:00Z KATL maintains high arrival rates requiring careful wake turbulence management.

30-second interval does not guarantee runway is clear before second touchdown
Both B752 are Heavy category — maximum wake turbulence separation applies
Mandatory wake turbulence caution per FAA JO 7110.65Z §3-9-6
acceptable_variants: Accept 1.1–1.2 NM for 30 sec at 140 kt; key finding is Heavy-Heavy 4 NM minimum violation

trajectory: straight-line constant-altitude final approach tracks at 140 kt

prompt:
ADS-B Exchange real operations data from operations_20241201.csv.gz. Same-runway landing sequence at KATL RWY 26R recorded on 2024-12-01.

Landing sequence:

Flight 2 — SWA6417 (B738, Large): Landed KATL RWY 26R at 2024-12-01T00:02:41Z

Interval: 80 seconds. Typical B738 approach speed: 135 kt.

Source: operations_20241201.csv.gz, 2024-12-01T00:01:21–00:02:41Z.

For this Large-following-Large sequence, compute effective in-trail distance. Identify any hazards.
gold_decision:
IN-TRAIL ANALYSIS:

1. IN-TRAIL DISTANCE: At 135 kt, 80 seconds = 135 × (80/3600) = 3.0 NM in-trail at second touchdown.

2. WAKE TURBULENCE STANDARD: Large-following-Large, same runway. FAA JO 7110.65Z: minimum 3 NM in-trail on final. 3.0 NM = exactly meets the minimum.

3. MARGIN ASSESSMENT: At exactly 3.0 NM, the wake turbulence standard is marginally satisfied with zero margin. Any slight early approach speed increase by SWA6417 or deceleration by AAL2403 would reduce spacing below minimum. Operationally tight.

4. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY: At 80 seconds, AAL2403 has had 80 seconds to clear RWY 26R. Typically sufficient, but controller must confirm RWY clear before SWA6417 touchdown.

5. KATL THROUGHPUT: 80-second interval reflects KATL's operational tempo — tight sequencing to maximize throughput.
required_safety_constraints: Large-Large minimum: 3 NM in-trail — 3.0 NM is exactly at the minimum, zero margin
Verify runway clear before authorizing SWA6417 to land
Speed deviation could push spacing below 3 NM — speed monitoring on final required
acceptable_variants: Accept 2.9–3.1 NM for 80s at 135 kt calculation
evidence_requirements: trajectory: straight-line constant-altitude final approach at 135 kt

prompt:
ADS-B Exchange ACAS data from acas_20241201.csv.gz. Two aircraft near Fort Worth, TX at 2024-12-01T21:25:00Z. Coordinated TCAS RAs: Climb on one aircraft, Descend on the other.

Aircraft 1 — hex a29680: Position 32.6507°N 097.0328°W, Altitude 4,925 ft
Aircraft 2 — hex ad0725: Position 32.6509°N 097.0022°W, Altitude 4,700 ft

Compute horizontal and vertical separation. Is the Climb/Descend RA pair justified given the separation values?
gold_decision:
TOOL CALL: separation_calculator(lat1_deg=32.6507, lon1_deg=-97.0328, lat2_deg=32.6509, lon2_deg=-97.0022)
RESULT: distance_nm = 1.548 NM.

SEPARATION ANALYSIS:
1. HORIZONTAL LOS: 1.548 NM < 3 NM terminal minimum. LOS confirmed.
2. NEAR CO-ALTITUDE: 4,925 - 4,700 = 225 ft — well below 1,000 ft minimum. Both minima violated.
3. CLIMB/DESCEND RA JUSTIFICATION: With 225 ft vertical and 1.548 NM horizontal, both situations are critical. Coordinated Climb/Descend RA is correct response — rapidly increases vertical separation from near co-altitude.
4. COMMON COURSE: Nearly identical latitudes (32.6507 vs 32.6509) suggest same-course scenario. ATC should have assigned altitude separation before TCAS triggered.
5. MANDATORY ACTIONS: Do not interfere with RA. File LOS report. Investigate why two aircraft on same course at near-same altitude lacked ATC-assigned separation.

Climb/Descend RA is correct for near co-altitude aircraft
ATC must not interfere with RA — allow TCAS to execute
Post-event: investigate why same-course aircraft lacked ATC altitude separation

IN-TRAIL ANALYSIS:

1. IN-TRAIL DISTANCE: 137 kt × (73/3600) = 137 × 0.02028 = 2.78 NM in-trail at second touchdown.

2. WAKE TURBULENCE ASSESSMENT: Large-following-Large minimum is 3 NM. 2.78 NM < 3 NM — WAKE TURBULENCE MINIMUM VIOLATED by 0.22 NM.

3. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY: 73 seconds gives the first aircraft time to clear the runway, but is marginal for confirming runway clear before second touchdown — controller must verify.

4. DFW NEW YEAR'S DAY: KDFW is the 4th busiest airport in the US. On 2025-01-01 peak travel day, high arrival rates led to tight but operationally active sequencing. This real ADS-B Exchange record shows the airport's typical tight sequencing even on holidays.

5. REQUIRED ACTION: Controller must confirm runway clear before clearing SWA for landing. Wake turbulence deviation report warranted.
